transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Sophia. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been thinking a lot about my job search lately. It feels like no matter how hard I try, I just can’t seem to get anywhere. It’s really frustrating. I don’t know, maybe I’m just not cut out for this?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling really frustrated with your job search, and it’s weighing on you. You mentioned that despite your efforts, you feel stuck and even question if you’re cut out for this. That must be really tough. Can you tell me more about what aspects of the job search have been the most challenging for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... I put so much effort into applying and preparing for interviews, but then I don’t hear back or get rejected. It feels like I’m doing everything wrong, even though I try to follow all the advice I find. I just keep thinking, “What’s the point?” It’s hard to stay motivated when it feels like nothing is working out. I’ve even stopped doing things I used to enjoy because I feel like I should be focusing on job hunting instead.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It sounds like you're really putting in a lot of effort and dedication into your job search, but the lack of responses and rejections are making you question your abilities and the value of your efforts. It's understandable that this would lead to feelings of discouragement and even impact your motivation to engage in activities you once enjoyed. That must feel quite heavy. 

When you think about the thoughts like “What’s the point?”—what emotions come up for you in those moments?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess when I think that way, I feel really anxious and scared. It’s like this weight on my chest, and I start to worry about the future. I feel lonely too, because I’ve cut out a lot of my social life to focus on the job search. It’s hard to reach out to friends when I feel like I’m failing at something so important. I just keep thinking that I should be doing better, and it’s hard to shake that feeling.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It sounds like the anxiety and fear about the future are really intense for you, especially with that weight on your chest. Feeling lonely because you've stepped back from your social life adds another layer to what you're experiencing. It’s understandable to feel like reaching out to friends is difficult when you’re grappling with these feelings of failure and pressure to do better. 

You mentioned thinking you should be doing better—can you share more about where that belief comes from? What does “doing better” look like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it comes from how I was raised. There was always this expectation to excel and achieve my goals. It feels like if I’m not succeeding in my job search, then I’m failing at life. “Doing better” means getting job offers, feeling confident in my skills, and just having a clear path ahead. But right now, it feels like I’m just wandering without direction, and that’s really hard for me to accept. I feel like I should have it all figured out by now.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It sounds like the expectations from your upbringing have created a strong pressure for you to excel and achieve, which makes the current uncertainty in your job search feel even more challenging. The idea that not succeeding in this area equates to failing at life is a heavy burden to carry. 

Feeling like you’re wandering without direction can be really unsettling, especially when you believe you should have everything figured out by now. It’s completely normal to feel lost at times, yet it seems like that’s hard for you to accept. 

How do you usually cope with those feelings of being lost or the pressure to succeed?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I tend to cope by being really critical of myself. I think if I push myself harder, maybe I’ll finally get the results I want. So, I over-prepare for interviews and spend hours perfecting my resume, but it often feels like it’s never enough. I also try to control everything around me, thinking that if I can just manage things better, I’ll get the outcome I want. But then, when things don’t go as planned, I just feel more hopeless and frustrated. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It sounds like being critical of yourself has become a way to cope with the pressure and uncertainty you’re feeling. You’re putting in a lot of effort to prepare and control the situation, hoping that it will lead to the results you desire. Yet, when things don’t go as planned, it only deepens your feelings of hopelessness and frustration, creating a cycle that’s hard to break. 

That must be exhausting, both mentally and emotionally. When you reflect on this cycle, what do you think it says about how you view yourself or your worth in relation to your job search?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it says a lot about how I see myself. I often tie my worth to my achievements, so when I’m not getting the results I want, it feels like I’m proving that I’m incompetent or a failure. It’s like I’m constantly measuring my value based on what I can accomplish, and when I don’t measure up, it just reinforces those negative beliefs about myself. I wish I could see my worth in a different way, but it’s really hard to break out of that mindset.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It sounds like your sense of self-worth is deeply intertwined with your achievements, which makes it incredibly difficult when things don’t go as planned. That belief that your value is tied to what you accomplish can create a lot of pressure and negative self-talk, especially during challenging times like your job search. 

Wanting to see your worth in a different way is a significant realization, and it’s understandable that breaking out of that mindset feels challenging. If you could imagine a different perspective on your worth, what might that look like for you? What would it mean to see yourself beyond your achievements?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think if I could see my worth beyond my achievements, it would mean recognizing that I have value just for being me—my qualities, my kindness, my ability to connect with others. It would mean accepting that it’s okay to struggle and that my worth isn’t diminished by setbacks. I guess it would involve being more compassionate with myself and understanding that everyone faces challenges. But, honestly, it feels like a big shift to make. I’m not sure how to get there, especially when I’m so used to measuring everything by success.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It’s a beautiful insight that you recognize your worth as being tied to your qualities and connections rather than just your achievements. Embracing self-compassion and accepting that it’s okay to struggle can indeed feel like a significant shift, especially when you’ve been conditioned to measure your value by success. 

It’s understandable to feel uncertain about how to make that transition. Change can be gradual, and it often starts with small steps. What do you think might be a gentle way to begin practicing that self-compassion or recognizing your worth outside of your achievements?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe starting with small reminders could help—like acknowledging my efforts, even if they don’t lead to the results I want. I could try to keep a journal where I write down things I like about myself or moments when I’ve been kind to others. It might also help to reach out to friends and share how I’m feeling instead of isolating myself. Just taking little steps to be kinder to myself could be a start. It feels a bit daunting, but I think it’s worth trying.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
Those sound like wonderful and thoughtful steps to take, Sophia. Acknowledging your efforts and keeping a journal to reflect on your qualities and kind moments can help shift your focus toward self-compassion. Reaching out to friends and sharing your feelings is also a great way to reconnect and remind yourself that you’re not alone in this.

It’s completely natural to feel a bit daunted by these changes, but it’s encouraging that you see the value in trying. How do you feel about starting with one of these steps? Which one resonates with you the most right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think starting with the journal feels like a good first step. It’s something I can do on my own, and it might help me process my thoughts and feelings without the pressure of sharing them right away. Just writing down positive things about myself or my efforts might help me see things from a different perspective. I’m a little nervous about it, but I think it could be a helpful way to start shifting my mindset.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
Starting with the journal sounds like a great choice, especially since it allows you to explore your thoughts and feelings at your own pace. It can be a safe space for you to reflect on the positive aspects of yourself and your efforts without any pressure. Feeling nervous is completely normal, but it’s encouraging that you see it as a helpful step toward shifting your mindset.

As you begin this journaling practice, what do you think might help you stay motivated to continue it, especially on days when it feels challenging?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think setting a specific time each day to write in my journal could help create a routine. Maybe I could start with just a few minutes, so it doesn’t feel overwhelming. I could also remind myself that it’s okay if some days are harder than others; the important thing is to keep trying. Maybe I could even reward myself for sticking with it, like treating myself to something small after a week of journaling. That might keep me motivated to keep going, even when it feels tough.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
Those are excellent strategies! Setting a specific time each day to write can help establish a routine, and starting with just a few minutes makes it feel more manageable. It’s also really kind of you to remind yourself that it’s okay to have ups and downs in the process. 

Rewarding yourself for sticking with it is a great way to celebrate your efforts and keep motivation high. It sounds like you’re creating a supportive framework for yourself as you embark on this journey. 

How do you feel about taking this first step? What’s your next move in getting started with your journaling practice?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit more hopeful about it, actually. I think I’ll set a specific time in the evening to write, maybe right before bed when things are quieter. I’ll start with just a few minutes, focusing on one or two things I appreciate about myself or my efforts that day. I’ll try to keep it simple and not put too much pressure on myself. Just getting started feels like a positive move, and I’m looking forward to seeing how it goes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
That sounds like a wonderful plan, Sophia! Setting aside time in the evening when things are quieter can create a peaceful space for reflection. Focusing on just one or two things each day keeps it simple and manageable, which is a great approach. 

Feeling hopeful about this step is a positive sign, and it’s encouraging to hear that you’re looking forward to it. Remember to be gentle with yourself as you start this practice. If you ever want to share how it’s going or explore anything else, I’m here to support you. Is there anything else on your mind today that you’d like to discuss?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate that. I think I feel a bit lighter after talking about this and making a plan. I don’t have anything else on my mind right now, but I’ll definitely keep in mind that I can share my progress or any other thoughts that come up. It feels good to have a small step to focus on. Thank you for your support!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m so glad to hear that you’re feeling a bit lighter and that our conversation has been helpful for you. Having a small step to focus on can make a big difference. Remember, it’s okay to take things one day at a time. I’m here whenever you want to share your progress or talk about anything else. Take care, and best of luck with your journaling!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much! I really appreciate your understanding and support. I’ll definitely keep you updated on how it goes. Take care, and I look forward to our next conversation!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You're very welcome, Sophia! I'm glad I could be here for you today. I look forward to hearing about your journey and how your journaling goes. Take care of yourself, and I’ll be here whenever you’re ready to chat again. Have a great day!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I hope you have a great day too. I’ll take care of myself and look forward to our next chat. Bye for now!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Thank you, Sophia! I appreciate that, and I’m looking forward to our next conversation as well. Take care, and goodbye for now!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Goodbye for now! Take care, and I’ll see you next time!
